content: When the 1st lord (lagna lord, ruler of body, vitality, and constitution) occupies the 1st house, the native classically tends toward a robust constitution and strong life-force. Parashara indicates such a native is generally healthy, of good physique, and inclined toward self-reliance in matters of the body. The lagna lord in its own house tends to support natural resilience and a tendency to recover quickly from minor disturbances. Because the ruler of vitality reinforces the house of the body, the constitution is often described as sturdy and the immune resilience as favorable. As always, this is a classical tendency toward vitality rather than a guarantee, and the overall strength, dignity, and afflictions of the lagna lord refine the picture.
source: Brihat Parashara Hora Shastra Ch.24 (Bhava Phala); Phaladeepika

content: When the 1st lord occupies the 2nd house (face, mouth, throat, eyes, and accumulated resources), the native's vitality becomes connected to nourishment, diet, and the regions of the face and throat. Classical texts associate this placement with attention to food and sustenance, and the constitution is often supported when eating habits are balanced. Sensitivities, if any, may classically tend to express around the mouth, teeth, throat, or eyes rather than systemically. The native generally directs personal energy toward securing comfort and resources, which can support steady well-being. This is a tendency-based reading: a well-placed and unafflicted 1st lord here supports good vitality, while affliction may suggest gentler care of diet and the facial region.
source: Brihat Parashara Hora Shastra Ch.24; Saravali

content: When the 1st lord occupies the 3rd house (courage, arms, shoulders, nervous energy, and effort), the native classically tends toward an active, energetic temperament and a vitality expressed through movement and enterprise. Parashara associates this placement with courage and self-effort, and the constitution is generally supported by physical activity and engagement. Because the 3rd house relates to the arms, shoulders, and the nervous and respiratory channels, sensitivities may classically tend to express in these areas when the lord is afflicted. The native's energy is strengthened by initiative and tends to wane with inactivity. As a tendency, a strong 1st lord here favors stamina and recovery, while affliction suggests mindful pacing of exertion.
source: Brihat Parashara Hora Shastra Ch.24; Phaladeepika

content: When the 1st lord occupies the 4th house (chest, heart region, emotional security, and home), the native's vitality classically becomes linked to emotional contentment and a stable home environment. Parashara associates this placement with happiness and comfort, and well-being is generally supported when the native feels emotionally settled. Because the 4th house relates to the chest and the emotional mind, sensitivities may classically tend toward the chest region or stress-related patterns when the lord is afflicted. A peaceful domestic life tends to reinforce the constitution, while domestic disturbance may unsettle it. As a tendency rather than a prediction, emotional balance and a nurturing environment are supportive factors for this placement's health.
source: Brihat Parashara Hora Shastra Ch.24; Saravali

content: When the 1st lord occupies the 5th house (stomach, digestion, intellect, and creativity), the native's vitality classically connects to the digestive region and the strength of the mind. Parashara associates this placement with intelligence and a generally fortunate disposition, which tends to support overall well-being. Because the 5th house relates to the upper abdomen and digestion, sensitivities may classically tend toward digestive sensitivity when the lord is afflicted. A lively, engaged mind generally reinforces the constitution here, and creative or joyful pursuits can be supportive of vitality. As a classical tendency, this is a favorable placement for life-force when the lord is dignified, with mindful attention to digestion advised under affliction.
source: Brihat Parashara Hora Shastra Ch.24; Phaladeepika

content: When the 1st lord occupies the 6th house (disease, recovery, immunity, and daily routine), the ruler of the body falls into the house of illness, which classical texts treat with caution regarding constitution. Parashara associates this placement with a tendency toward periodic ailments or a sensitive constitution, though the 6th is also the house of recovery and the capacity to overcome disease. Importantly, because the 6th is an upachaya (house of growth), the native often builds health resilience over time and can develop strong recuperative power. The native may benefit from disciplined routine, attention to immunity, and proactive self-care. This is framed strictly as a classical tendency toward health-vulnerability that is balanced by an equally classical capacity to recover and overcome, not as any prediction of serious illness.
source: Brihat Parashara Hora Shastra Ch.24; Saravali

content: When the 1st lord occupies the 7th house (partnership, the lower abdomen and pelvic region, and public dealings), the native's vitality classically becomes intertwined with relationships and the energy invested in partnerships. Parashara associates this placement with engagement in worldly and relational affairs, and well-being is generally supported when relational life is harmonious. Because the 7th house relates to the lower abdominal and pelvic region, sensitivities may classically tend toward that region when the lord is afflicted. Relational stress can affect vitality here, while a balanced partnership tends to be supportive. As a tendency rather than a diagnosis, harmony in close relationships is a meaningful supporting factor for this placement's constitution.
source: Brihat Parashara Hora Shastra Ch.24; Phaladeepika

content: When the 1st lord occupies the 8th house (chronic matters, longevity, transformation, and hidden processes), the ruler of the body falls into a house of deep change, which classical texts approach with care regarding the constitution. Parashara associates this placement with a constitution that may be more sensitive and benefits from steady, mindful self-care. The 8th house also relates to longevity and recuperative depth, so a strong and dignified 1st lord here can confer notable endurance and the capacity to recover from significant challenges. The native may be drawn to understanding the deeper or hidden aspects of health and wellness. This is presented as a classical tendency toward a sensitive but potentially resilient constitution, emphasizing care and prevention rather than any prediction of serious or terminal outcomes.
source: Brihat Parashara Hora Shastra Ch.24; Saravali

content: When the 1st lord occupies the 9th house (fortune, dharma, the hips and thighs, and higher purpose), the native's vitality is classically supported by good fortune and an optimistic, principled outlook. Parashara associates this placement with fortune, virtue, and a generally favorable disposition, which tends to reinforce overall well-being. Because the 9th house relates to the hips and thighs, sensitivities may classically tend toward that region when the lord is afflicted. A sense of meaning, faith, and ethical living tends to support the constitution here, and the native often enjoys protective good fortune in matters of vitality. As a classical tendency, this is generally regarded as a supportive placement for life-force when the lord is well-disposed.
source: Brihat Parashara Hora Shastra Ch.24; Phaladeepika

content: When the 1st lord occupies the 10th house (career, public life, the knees, and worldly action), the native's vitality is classically directed into work and achievement. Parashara associates this placement with an active, accomplished life and engagement in meaningful action, and well-being is generally supported when work is satisfying and balanced. Because the 10th house relates to the knees and the structural joints, sensitivities may classically tend toward that region when the lord is afflicted. Overexertion or chronic overwork can tax vitality here, so balance between effort and rest is a supportive factor. As a tendency rather than a prediction, a dignified 1st lord in the 10th favors an energetic constitution sustained by purposeful activity.
source: Brihat Parashara Hora Shastra Ch.24; Saravali

content: When the 1st lord occupies the 11th house (gains, fulfillment, the calves and ankles, and social networks), the native's vitality is classically supported by the fulfillment of aspirations and a strong sense of social belonging. Parashara associates this placement with gains, friends, and the achievement of goals, conditions that tend to reinforce well-being and morale. Because the 11th house relates to the lower legs, calves, and ankles, sensitivities may classically tend toward that region when the lord is afflicted. The 11th being an upachaya house, the native often experiences improving vitality and circumstances over time. As a classical tendency, this is generally viewed as a supportive placement for life-force, with steady improvement and good social support sustaining the constitution.
source: Brihat Parashara Hora Shastra Ch.24; Phaladeepika

content: When the 1st lord occupies the 12th house (rest, seclusion, the feet, expenditure of energy, and foreign or hidden environments), classical texts counsel attention to the conservation of vitality. Parashara associates this placement with expenditure and a tendency for energy to be spent or drained, so adequate rest, sleep, and restoration are supportive factors here. Because the 12th house relates to the feet and to subtle nervous energy, sensitivities may classically tend toward those areas or toward fatigue when the lord is afflicted. The 12th is also the house of recuperation, retreat, and healing environments, so periods of rest and convalescence can be genuinely restorative for this native. This is framed as a classical tendency emphasizing rest and energy conservation, not as any prediction of illness; mindful self-care and sufficient sleep are the supportive themes.
source: Brihat Parashara Hora Shastra Ch.24; Saravali
